---
title: "Types & extractors"
toc: true
---

Every GoogleMapsAPI response is a raw JSON object (`JSON.Object`) — great
for exploratory access via dot notation, but awkward to hand to the wider
geospatial ecosystem.

GoogleMapsAPI.jl ships four lightweight wrapper types plus a set of extractor
functions that normalise *any* response shape into the same structure.

## The wrapper types

```{julia}
using GoogleMapsAPI
```

### `GMPoint`

A single lat/lng pair.

```{julia}
p = GMPoint(37.4, -122.1)
```

### `GMLineString`

An ordered sequence of `GMPoint`s — produced whenever a response carries an
encoded polyline (e.g. a route from `compute_routes`).

```{julia}
ls = GMLineString([GMPoint(0.0, 0.0), GMPoint(1.0, 2.0), GMPoint(3.0, 4.0)])
length(ls), collect(ls)
```

### `GMFeature`

Geometry (a `GMPoint`, `GMLineString`, or `nothing`) paired with the raw
response object that yielded it — so you keep access to fields like
`formatted_address`, `placeId`, `name`, `elevation`, `duration`, etc.

```{julia}
f = GMFeature(p, (; formatted_address = "Mountain View, CA"))
f.geometry, f.properties
```

### `GMFeatureCollection`

Iterable container of `GMFeature`s with `length`, indexing, and iteration.

```{julia}
fc = GMFeatureCollection([f, GMFeature(nothing, (; note = "no geometry"))])
length(fc), fc[1].geometry
```

## `features(response)` — normalise any response

`features` walks any GoogleMapsAPI return value and produces a
`GMFeatureCollection`. It handles:

- `geocode` / `reverse_geocode` — `results[i].geometry.location` → points.
- `compute_routes` — `routes[i].polyline.encodedPolyline` → linestrings.
- `places`, `places_nearby`, `find_place` — `results`/`candidates[i].geometry.location`.
- `place` — single `result.geometry.location`.
- `places_autocomplete` — `predictions` (no geometry, just properties).
- `elevation`, `snap_to_roads`, `nearest_roads` — bare arrays of
  `{location, …}` objects.
- `geolocate` — single `{location, accuracy}`.
- Any object with an unrecognised shape is wrapped as a single-feature
  collection with `nothing` geometry and the raw object as properties.

```{julia}
using JSON

# Synthetic geocode-shaped response for illustration
r = JSON.parse("""
{
    "status":"OK",
    "results":[
        {"geometry":{"location":{"lat":37.4,"lng":-122.1}},"formatted_address":"A"},
        {"geometry":{"location":{"lat":40.0,"lng":-74.0}},"formatted_address":"B"}
    ]
}
""")

fc = features(r)
```

```{julia}
fc[1].geometry
```

```{julia}
fc[1].properties.formatted_address
```

## Convenience extractors

For the common cases you don't need the full feature wrapper — just the
points or the polylines.

```{julia}
points(r)
```

```{julia}
route = JSON.parse("""{"routes":[{"polyline":{"encodedPolyline":"_p~iF~ps|U_ulLnnqC_mqNvxq`@"}}]}""")
linestrings(route)
```

```{julia}
geometries(r)
```

## Why wrappers?

Three reasons:

1. **Type stability.** `GMPoint` is a concrete struct — accessing `p.lat`
   costs nothing. Walking into a `JSON.Object` hits dynamic dispatch.
2. **GeoInterface.** These wrapper types implement
   [GeoInterface.jl](https://github.com/JuliaGeo/GeoInterface.jl) traits, so
   any GeoInterface-aware package (GeoMakie, GeoJSON, ArchGDAL, Shapefile,
   GeometryOps, …) can consume GoogleMapsAPI responses directly. See
   [Plotting](plotting.qmd).
3. **Normalisation.** `features(x)` lets downstream code iterate uniformly
   without branching on which endpoint produced `x`.
